advaita.rendering: Brahman — the supreme knowable (jneya) — is anadi (without beginning) because it is not constituted by any prior cause; it transcends the reach of the words 'sat' (being) and 'asat' (non-being) because all words function only through the fourfold nexus of jati (class), kriya (action), guna (quality), or sambandha (relation), none of which apply to Brahman. This is not a contradiction: Brahman is neither the object of the cognition 'it is' nor 'it is not,' being beyond the senses and thus beyond both forms of buddhi (cognition). The Upanishads confirm: 'neti neti' — Brahman is designated only by negation, as that which is 'other than the known and above the unknown' (Kena 1.3).
advaita.bhashya_anchor: Shankara addresses at length why 'na sat tan nasad ucyate' is not a contradiction: Brahman is beyond indriya-gamya (sense-accessible) objects and thus beyond both asti-buddhi and nasti-buddhi.
advaita.divergence_note: Shankara explicitly rejects the alternative reading 'anadimad mat-param' (taking 'mat' as referring to Vasudeva-shakti) as apavyakhyana — a distorted reading — because attributing a specific shakti to Brahman violates the total negation of vishesha that this verse establishes.

vishishtadvaita.rendering: The knowable is parasuddha-atman — the perfectly pure self — which, though itself without hands and feet in the ordinary sense, is the inner controller (antaryamin) capable of the function of all hands, feet, eyes, and ears everywhere. This Brahman pervades (avrtya tishthati) all that exists in the cosmos, yet remains spatially unlimited (desha-pariccheda-rahita). The liberated soul, on attaining 'sadharmmyam' with Bhagavan (Gita 14.2), itself attains this all-pervasive functional omnipotence — a fact grounded in shruti: 'sa ya evam vit... niranjanah paramam samyam upaiti' (Mundaka 3.1.3).
vishishtadvaita.bhashya_anchor: Ramanuja's bhashya on this verse pivots from anādimat to the all-pervasiveness (sarvato-pani-pada) described in the following verse, treating the knowable as the pure self (vishishta-atman) that subsumes both Ishvara and jiva.
vishishtadvaita.divergence_note: Ramanuja does not read 'na sat tan nasad' as an absolute negation of all predication; rather, Brahman transcends ordinary sat/asat because its nature as vishishta (qualified by cit and acit) exceeds the binary. Brahman is supremely real, not beyond reality.

dvaita.rendering: The 'param Brahma' here is Hari alone — the supreme independent Lord (svantantra) established by the preceding verse's promise ('sa ca yah,' 13.4). 'Anadimad' signals that Brahman has no external cause or prior: unlike the body which is 'adimad' (having a beginning), Brahman is eternal and self-caused. Hari's 'anyatya-prabhava' — His distinctive potency unlike any other — is precisely what is indicated. The jiva, by contrast, is always 'anyadhina' (dependent), utterly distinct and perpetually subordinate to Hari.
dvaita.bhashya_anchor: Madhva's commentary is terse: he links this verse to the pratijna of 13.4 and glosses 'anadimad' as 'adimad-deha-varjitam' — that which is without the body-bound beginnings that characterize bound souls.
dvaita.divergence_note: Madhva does not engage the sat/asat dialectic at length; for him, Brahman's transcendence of sat/asat means only that ordinary categories of finite existence do not capture Hari's infinite svabhava — not that Brahman is beyond existence as such.

shuddhadvaita.rendering: The knowable Brahman (jneya) is Krishna himself, disclosed through six groups of his own svarupa-gunas (qualities intrinsic to his nature). 'Anadimad' means: Krishna as para-Brahman is the foundation of all Vedanta; there is no prior to him, for he is sva-prakasha (self-luminous). The phrase 'na sat tan nasad ucyate' reveals that Krishna's svarupa is the ashraya (ground) of two apparently contradictory dharmas — being and non-being — yet neither exhausts him: as Bhagavata proclaims, 'na hi virodha ubhayam bhagavaty apariganita-guna-gana Ishvare' — there is no contradiction in the Lord of uncountable gunas. Krishna's nature exceeds all categorical containment.
shuddhadvaita.bhashya_anchor: Vallabha reads jneya through the Bhagavata's positive attribution of infinite gunas to Ishvara, treating 'na sat na asat' not as negation of all qualities but as marking the limit of binary categorization before Krishna's ananta (infinite) guna-ocean.
shuddhadvaita.divergence_note: Vallabha diverges sharply from Shankara: Brahman is not nirvishesha (featureless) but svishesha (fully qualified) — the 'na sat na asat' formula is a floor, not a ceiling; it rules out finite categories while leaving Krishna's positive gunas intact.

bhakti.rendering: The knowable (jneya) is para-nirvishesha Brahman — Vishnu's transcendent, featureless aspect — which the seeker who desires moksha should know through the sadhanas enumerated in prior verses. It is 'anadimad': the compound either reads as a bahuvrihi (that which has no adi, i.e., beginning) making the matup suffix metrically redundant, or it splits as two words — 'anadih' and 'mat-param' — meaning 'my (Vishnu's) supreme featureless rupa.' The verse then says 'na sat na asat': what is posited by vidhi-pramana (injunctive knowledge) is called sat; what is posited by nishedha-pramana (prohibitive knowledge) is asat; this jneya, being the ground of both without being captured by either, is described only by exclusion.
bhakti.bhashya_anchor: Sridhara explicitly presents the two-reading option on 'anadimad' and favors the mat-param reading as denoting Vishnu's own nirvishesha-brahma-rupa. He follows Shankara's framework for 'na sat na asat' but inflects it devotionally.
bhakti.divergence_note: Sridhara's 'mat-param' reading points toward a personal ground for the impersonal Brahman — Vishnu is the named antecedent of 'mat' — which Shankara rejects as apavyakhyana. The devotional school thus retains a personal anchor even within the apophatic frame.

advaita-bhakti.rendering: The jneya is nirvishesha-brahma-vastu — the objectless pure-consciousness object — about which Krishna promises clarity (pravakshyami). 'Anadimad' is read by some as 'anadih mat-param' (my supreme formless aspect) — but Madhusudana explicitly rejects the alternative reading 'aham Vasudeva-akhya para shaktir yasya' as apavyakhyana, since this verse's entire purpose is to negate all vishesha in Brahman. 'Na sat na asat' means: 'sat' is what is the object of vidhi-pramana (affirmative cognition), 'asat' is what is the object of nishedha-pramana (negative cognition); Brahman, as svaprakasha-chaitanya (self-luminous consciousness) beyond both, falls under neither. All four modes of word-application — by jati, kriya, guna, sambandha — are unavailable, hence 'yato vacho nivartante' (Taittiriya 2.4.9).
advaita-bhakti.bhashya_anchor: Madhusudana provides the most technically elaborate treatment, cataloguing all four pravrtti-nimittas of shabda (word) and showing that each is absent in Brahman. He synthesizes Shankara's dialectic with a positive framing: lakshana (secondary denotation) is still possible, enabling shastric transmission even of the unspeakable.
advaita-bhakti.divergence_note: Madhusudana's synthesis holds: Brahman is nirvishesha (Shankara), yet Krishna can 'pravakshyami' (speak it) through lakshana-vrtti — indirect denotation. Bhakti-rasa is not surrendered; the devotee's Krishna is the very svarupa that transcends both sat and asat.

so_what_questions: If Brahman cannot be called 'sat' (existing) or 'asat' (non-existing), what epistemic status does the Gita's own claim to teach about it actually have?
Shankara says words function only via jati, kriya, guna, or sambandha — and none applies to Brahman. Is the Gita itself then pointing at its own limit?
When Ramanuja says Brahman transcends sat/asat because it is 'vishishta' rather than nirvishesha, is that a different answer or a different question from Shankara's?
If Madhva's Hari is beyond sat/asat while remaining the fully independent Lord with definite svabhava, does 'na sat na asat' mean something categorically different for Dvaita than for Advaita?
Vallabha's Krishna is the ashraya (ground) of both contradictory dharmas — does this make 'na sat na asat' a sign of abundance rather than absence?
Madhusudana allows lakshana-vrtti (indirect denotation) to rescue scriptural transmission of the unspeakable — does that rescue apply to all schools equally, or only to the nirvishesha position?
If the knowable (jneya) is that by knowing which one attains amritatvam, and the knowable is beyond all predication, what does 'knowing' it actually consist in?

everyday_applications.advaita: When you catch yourself insisting 'I know exactly what I am' or 'I know exactly what I am not' — career, role, relationship — notice that both the affirmation and the negation are equally incomplete. The jñani's practice here is to hold the open question without collapsing it: neither claiming full self-knowledge nor denying that there is something to know.
everyday_applications.vishishtadvaita: Your deepest self is not the bounded person you experience on an ordinary day. Ramanuja's point is that the pure atman, when liberated, participates in Bhagavan's all-pervasive functioning. In practice: when you act in service (kainkarya), stop tracking the limits of your capacity — act as though the Lord's hands and eyes are operating through you, because the shruti says they can.
everyday_applications.dvaita: You are not Hari. This is not a humiliation — it is the architecture of relationship. Madhva's application: in work, in family, in practice — identify clearly what is your responsibility and what is the Lord's domain. Dependency on Hari is not weakness; it is the accurate map of how reality is structured. Confusing yourself with the source of power is the root error.
everyday_applications.shuddhadvaita: The verse's 'na sat na asat' points to Krishna's inexhaustibility. Vallabha's application: do not reduce your encounter with Krishna — in scripture, image, kirtan, or memory — to a single fixed meaning. The Lord's gunas are ananta (infinite); every encounter can reveal a new facet. The devotee's practice is to stay porous to that inexhaustibility rather than fixing a final definition.
everyday_applications.bhakti: Sridhara's 'mat-param' reading keeps a personal anchor in the impersonal: even nirvishesha-Brahman is 'Vishnu's own rupa.' In life, this means: intellectual inquiry into the absolute need not end in abstraction — it can return to a personal devotional center. When philosophical study exhausts the conceptual categories, let it empty into bhakti rather than into nihilism.
everyday_applications.advaita-bhakti: Madhusudana's synthesis offers a practical technology for moments of genuine unknowing: when you cannot name what you are experiencing — grief that is not quite grief, joy that exceeds its occasion — resist the urgency to categorize. The experience is pointing beyond both the affirmative and negative frames. This is not pathology; it is the self touching its own ground. The appropriate response is not analysis but attentive stillness.

self_assessment: Six genuinely divergent renderings delivered, each anchored in supplied bhashya prose; sat/asat dialectic receives distinct treatment in every school (negation-of-vishesha in Advaita, vishishta-ground in Vishishtadvaita, svantantra-svabhava in Dvaita, ananta-guna-abundance in Shuddhadvaita, vidhi/nishedha-pramana in Bhakti, lakshana-vrtti rescue in Advaita-bhakti); everyday-applications are school-specific with no cross-school collapse.
